title: Verilator `+verilator+seed` Bugs
body:
## Documentation Claim

From the Verilator docs (`docs/guide/exe_sim.rst`):

> **+verilator+seed+\<value\>**: For `$random` and `--x-initial unique`, set
> the simulation runtime random seed value. If zero or not specified picks a
> value from the system random number generator.

Two bugs prevent this from working as documented.

---

## Bug 1: Cannot pass 0 as seed value

Passing `+verilator+seed+0` on the command line to request random seeding (as documented) produces a fatal error instead of enabling random behavior. Users who follow the documentation and use `0` to mean "pick a seed randomly" cannot do so.

The command-line argument parser enforces a minimum value of `1`:

**`include/verilated.cpp:3088-3090`**
```cpp
} else if (commandArgVlUint64(arg,
    "+verilator+seed+", u64, 1, std::numeric_limits<int>::max())) {
    randSeed(static_cast<int>(u64));
```

The `min` parameter passed to `commandArgVlUint64` is `1`, so passing
`+verilator+seed+0` produces:

```
%Error: COMMAND_LINE:0: Argument '+verilator+seed+' must be an unsigned
integer, greater than 0, less than 2147483648
```

Internally, the seed **does** default to `0` and the "0 means use system RNG"
logic exists:

**`include/verilated.h:393`**
```cpp
int m_randSeed = 0;  // Random seed: 0=random
```

**`include/verilated.cpp:3154-3160`**
```cpp
uint64_t VerilatedContextImp::randSeedDefault64() const VL_MT_SAFE {
    if (randSeed() != 0) {
        return ((static_cast<uint64_t>(randSeed()) << 32) ^
                (static_cast<uint64_t>(randSeed())));
    } else {
        return ((static_cast<uint64_t>(vl_sys_rand32()) << 32) ^
                (static_cast<uint64_t>(vl_sys_rand32())));
    }
}
```

**Fix**: Change the `min` on line 3088 from `1` to `0`.

---

## Bug 2: System RNG is never seeded, producing deterministic output

When no `+verilator+seed` is specified, the simulation produces the **same results on every run**. Users who rely on the default behavior to get non-deterministic simulation — for example, to randomize initial register values or expose different corner cases across runs — will instead get an identical execution every time, silently defeating the purpose of random testing.

When no `+verilator+seed` is provided (or if seed is `0`),
`randSeedDefault64()` falls through to `vl_sys_rand32()`, which uses
`lrand48()`:

**`include/verilated.cpp:411-422`**
```cpp
static uint32_t vl_sys_rand32() VL_MT_SAFE {
    static VerilatedMutex s_mutex;
    const VerilatedLockGuard lock{s_mutex};
#if defined(_WIN32) && !defined(__CYGWIN__)
    return (std::rand() << 16) ^ std::rand();
#else
    return (lrand48() << 16) ^ lrand48();
#endif
}
```

Per the POSIX specification, `lrand48()` uses a default seed of `0` when
`srand48()` has not been called. Since Verilator never calls `srand48()` with a
non-deterministic value (e.g. from `time()`, `getpid()`, or `/dev/urandom`),
`vl_sys_rand32()` returns the same sequence on every run.

This means the documentation's claim that omitting the seed "picks a value from
the system random number generator" is misleading — it does call `lrand48()`,
but that RNG itself is never randomized.

**Fix**: Seed `lrand48()` with a non-deterministic source during initialization
of `vl_sys_rand32()` (e.g. `srand48(time(nullptr) ^ getpid())`), or replace
`lrand48()` with a source that is inherently non-deterministic (e.g. reading
from `/dev/urandom`).
